introSection: The Airplane Coaster, also formerly known as the Aero-Coaster, Aeroplane Dips, and Coaster-Dips, was a Wooden roller coaster formerly located in Playland Park in Rye, New York, United States. The ride was known for its incredible intensity, which is what would eventually lead to its closure.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
When the ride was in planning, it was to be called Bob, like most of Church's coasters. However, it was changed to Aeroplane-Dips to commemorate Charles Lindbergh's historic flight from New York to Paris. After opening in 1928, it was renamed Aero-Coaster, then Coaster-Dips, and finally, and most well-known, the Airplane Coaster.

While it was an extremely popular ride, maintenance issues were present right at the start. The structure was not capable of handling the fast turns and banked hills and dips, and this caused keeping up with the coaster difficult overtime as it aged.

In November of 1957, the coaster was torn down after insurance companies told Playland in 1956 that it was "not very safe to ride".

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The ride takes many inspirations from the Bobs formerly located at Riverview Park, with its intense layout and banked curves.

fullText:
The Airplane Coaster, also formerly known as the Aero-Coaster, Aeroplane Dips, and Coaster-Dips, was a Wooden roller coaster formerly located in Playland Park in Rye, New York, United States. The ride was known for its incredible intensity, which is what would eventually lead to its closure.

When the ride was in planning, it was to be called Bob, like most of Church's coasters. However, it was changed to Aeroplane-Dips to commemorate Charles Lindbergh's historic flight from New York to Paris. After opening in 1928, it was renamed Aero-Coaster, then Coaster-Dips, and finally, and most well-known, the Airplane Coaster.

While it was an extremely popular ride, maintenance issues were present right at the start. The structure was not capable of handling the fast turns and banked hills and dips, and this caused keeping up with the coaster difficult overtime as it aged.

In November of 1957, the coaster was torn down after insurance companies told Playland in 1956 that it was "not very safe to ride".

The ride takes many inspirations from the Bobs formerly located at Riverview Park, with its intense layout and banked curves.
